questions.question: State the recurrence relation for the Merge Sort algorithm and name the Master Theorem case that applies to it.
expectedPoints: This matches Case 2 of the Master Theorem (where k=0).
Therefore, the solution is T(n) = Θ(n log n).

questions.question: Compare and contrast the Quick Sort and Merge Sort algorithms based on their use of the Divide and Conquer strategy. Discuss their worst-case time complexity and the scenarios that lead to it.
expectedPoints: Both are Divide and Conquer sorting algorithms.
Merge Sort: Division is simple (midpoint). Combining (merging) is complex (O(n)). Always has a time complexity of Θ(n log n).
Quick Sort: Division (partitioning) is complex (O(n), finding pivot placement). Combining is trivial. Worst-case complexity is O(n²).
Worst-case for QuickSort: Occurs when the pivot is consistently the smallest or largest element, leading to highly unbalanced partitions (e.g., when the input is already sorted or reverse-sorted).
Stability: Merge Sort is stable, Quick Sort is typically not.
Space Complexity: Merge Sort requires O(n) auxiliary space, Quick Sort is in-place (O(log n) space for recursion stack).
